stem: Let \( \ell \) be the line in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) spanned by \( \mathbf{u} = ([[u1]], [[u2]], [[u3]]) \). The reflection of \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]], [[v2]], [[v3]]) \) across \( \ell \) is \( \mathbf{v}' = 2\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf{u}}(\mathbf{v}) - \mathbf{v} \). What is the first coordinate of \( \mathbf{v}' \)?
solution: The projection of \( \mathbf{v} \) onto \( \mathbf{u} \) has first coordinate \( \dfrac{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{u}}{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{u}}u_1 \); the reflection is \( 2\,\text{proj} - \mathbf{v} \), so its first coordinate is \( 2\cdot\dfrac{([[dotvu]])}{[[uu]]}\cdot([[u1]]) - ([[v1]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds \( \mathbf{v} \) instead of subtracting it in \( 2\,\text{proj} - \mathbf{v} \).
This drops the factor of 2 on the projection term.
This gives the projection's first coordinate, not the reflection's.
This returns the original first coordinate, as if there were no reflection.
This negates the original coordinate instead of reflecting across the line.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This has the wrong sign.

stem: Let \( \Pi \) be the plane through the origin in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \) with normal vector \( \mathbf{n} = ([[n1]], [[n2]], [[n3]]) \). The reflection of \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]], [[v2]], [[v3]]) \) across \( \Pi \) is \( \mathbf{v}' = \mathbf{v} - 2\dfrac{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{n}}{\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{n}}\mathbf{n} \). What is the first coordinate of \( \mathbf{v}' \)?
solution: The reflection across a plane with normal \( \mathbf{n} \) subtracts twice the projection onto \( \mathbf{n} \): the first coordinate is \( ([[v1]]) - 2\cdot\dfrac{([[dotvn]])}{[[nn]]}\cdot([[n1]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the projection term instead of subtracting it.
This drops the factor of 2 on the projection term.
This is the projection of \( \mathbf{v} \) onto \( \mathbf{n} \), not the reflection across the plane.
This returns the original first coordinate, as if there were no reflection.
This negates the original coordinate instead of reflecting across the plane.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This has the wrong sign.

stem: Let \( P = \dfrac{\mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^T}{\mathbf{u}^T\mathbf{u}} \) be the matrix that orthogonally projects onto the line spanned by \( \mathbf{u} = ([[u1]], [[u2]], [[u3]]) \) in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \). What is the entry \( P_{12} \) (row 1, column 2) of \( P \)?
solution: The \( (i,j) \) entry of \( P = \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^T/(\mathbf{u}^T\mathbf{u}) \) is \( u_iu_j/(\mathbf{u}^T\mathbf{u}) \), so \( P_{12} = \dfrac{([[u1]])([[u2]])}{[[uu]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the diagonal entry \( P_{22} \), not the off-diagonal entry \( P_{12} \).
This is the diagonal entry \( P_{11} \), not \( P_{12} \).
This is the entry \( P_{13} \), pairing the wrong components.
This forgets to divide by \( \mathbf{u}^T\mathbf{u} \).
This is off by one from the correct entry.
This is off by one from the correct entry.
This has the wrong sign.

stem: Let \( P = \mathbf{u}\mathbf{u}^T/(\mathbf{u}^T\mathbf{u}) \) and let \( R = 2P - I \) be the matrix that reflects vectors across the line spanned by \( \mathbf{u} = ([[u1]], [[u2]], [[u3]]) \) in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \). What is the diagonal entry \( R_{11} \) of \( R \)?
solution: Since \( R = 2P - I \), the diagonal entry is \( R_{11} = 2P_{11} - 1 = 2\cdot\dfrac{([[u1]])^2}{[[uu]]} - 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to subtract \( 1 \) for the identity term.
This forgets the factor of \( 2 \) on \( P_{11} \).
This is just the projection entry \( P_{11} \), not the reflection entry.
This has the sign of the whole expression flipped.
This is off by one from the correct entry.
This is off by one from the correct entry.
This has the wrong sign.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{u}_1 = ([[a]], [[b]], 0) \) and \( \mathbf{u}_2 = (-[[b]], [[a]], [[c]]) \); these are orthogonal vectors in \( \mathbb{R}^3 \), and let \( W = \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{u}_1, \mathbf{u}_2\} \). Because \( \mathbf{u}_1 \perp \mathbf{u}_2 \), the projection of \( \mathbf{v} = ([[v1]], [[v2]], [[v3]]) \) onto \( W \) is the SUM of the individual projections: \( \operatorname{proj}_W(\mathbf{v}) = \operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf{u}_1}(\mathbf{v}) + \operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf{u}_2}(\mathbf{v}) \). What is the first coordinate of \( \operatorname{proj}_W(\mathbf{v}) \)?
solution: Since \( \mathbf{u}_1\perp\mathbf{u}_2 \), add the two rank-one projections' first coordinates: \( \dfrac{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{u}_1}{\mathbf{u}_1\cdot\mathbf{u}_1}([[a]]) + \dfrac{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{u}_2}{\mathbf{u}_2\cdot\mathbf{u}_2}(-[[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This includes only the projection onto \( \mathbf{u}_1 \), forgetting the \( \mathbf{u}_2 \) contribution.
This includes only the projection onto \( \mathbf{u}_2 \), forgetting the \( \mathbf{u}_1 \) contribution.
This subtracts the two projections instead of adding them.
This returns the original first coordinate, as if \( \mathbf{v} \) were unchanged by the projection.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This has the wrong sign.
